required_record_contract.summary: include caveats, disconfirming evidence, and mapping limits

items.design: 2x2: condition A = committed BG gate active, condition B = gate ablated (beta suppressed), crossed with stable vs volatile environment. 2 matched seeds. PASS: harm_committed < harm_ablated in stable env; gap narrows or reverses in volatile env.
items.notes: Depends on ARC-016 (provisional) and MECH-090 (active). This is the behavioral consequence layer split from ARC-016 into ARC-029 during 2026-03-22 governance session.
items.objective: Test whether committed operating mode produces measurably lower harm outcomes than ablated (uncommitted) mode, and whether this gap narrows in volatile environments (gate-ablation design).

key_sources: Balleine & O'Doherty (2010) - Human and rodent homologues in action control: corticostriatal determinants of goal-directed and habitual action
DLS/DMS lesion studies distinguishing outcome-sensitive vs stimulus-response behavior
Beta oscillation persistence during committed action sequences (Khanna & Carmena 2015 or similar)
Daw et al. (2005) - Uncertainty-based competition between prefrontal and dorsolateral striatal systems for behavioral control
items.objective: Ground ARC-029 in habitual vs goal-directed neuroscience: dorsolateral vs dorsomedial striatum dissociation, beta persistence during habitual sequences, model-free/model-based dual-process accounts.

key_sources: Bacon, Harb & Precup (2017) - The Option-Critic Architecture
Vezhnevets et al. (2017) - FeUdal Networks for Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning
Daw, Gershman et al. (2011) - Model-based influences on humans choices and striatal prediction errors (two-step task)
items.objective: Ground ARC-029 in hierarchical RL and options frameworks: option-critic architecture, Feudal Networks, two-step task model-based switching.

key_sources: Gallese et al. (1996) / Rizzolatti & Craighero (2004) - Mirror neuron system and action understanding
Kording & Wolpert (2004) - Bayesian integration in sensorimotor learning (causal inference framework)
Decety & Lamm (2007) - The role of the right temporoparietal junction in social interaction
Wolpert, Ghahramani & Jordan (1995) - An internal model for sensorimotor integration (efference copy)
items.objective: Ground MECH-072 (foreseeable-harm gating reduces false attribution) in causal attribution neuroscience: mirror neuron agency detection, Bayesian causal inference, TPJ role in intentional attribution, cerebellar efference copy in self/other discrimination.

key_sources: Hafner et al. (2020) - Dream to Control: Learning Behaviors by Latent Imagination (Dreamer)
Scholkopf et al. (2021) - Toward Causal Representation Learning
Pathak et al. (2017) - Curiosity-driven Exploration by Self-supervised Prediction (self/world separation in ICM)
items.objective: Ground MECH-072 in ML causal attribution and world model separation: Dreamer-style world model, causal representation learning, curiosity-driven exploration.

items.design: Condition A = harm-surprise-gated beta variance (unexpected harm spike triggers variance increase). Condition B = jitter-only control (no harm-triggered spike). PASS: A shows significantly higher beta_variance on unexpected harm events than B; B shows stable low-level jitter only.
items.notes: EXQ-049e PASS validated Route-1 (noise floor preventing variance collapse). This experiment validates Route-2 (harm-surprise triggering volatility spike). Both routes needed for full MECH-104 validation.
items.objective: Validate Route-2 MECH-104 wiring: unexpected harm events should spike commitment uncertainty (beta variance) via surprise gate, distinct from the jitter noise floor baseline.

key_sources: Yu & Dayan (2005) - Uncertainty, neuromodulation and attention (expected vs unexpected uncertainty)
Aston-Jones & Cohen (2005) - An integrative theory of locus coeruleus-norepinephrine function: adaptive gain and optimal performance
Sara (2009) - The locus coeruleus and noradrenergic modulation of cognition
Bouret & Sara (2005) - Network reset: a simplified overarching theory of locus coeruleus noradrenaline function
items.objective: Deepen biological grounding of MECH-104 (LC-NE volatility interrupt): Yu & Dayan 2005 expected/unexpected uncertainty distinction, Aston-Jones & Cohen adaptive gain theory, Sara norepinephrine and memory consolidation, Bouret & Sara network reset hypothesis.

key_sources: Nassar et al. (2010) - An approximately Bayesian delta-rule model explains the dynamics of belief updating in a changing environment
Behrens et al. (2007) - Learning the value of information in an uncertain world
Mathys et al. (2011) - A Bayesian foundation for individual learning under uncertainty (HGF)
Adams & MacKay (2007) - Bayesian online changepoint detection
items.objective: Ground MECH-104 in computational uncertainty and change-point detection: Nassar surprise-gated learning, Behrens volatility inference, Mathys hierarchical Gaussian filter, Adams & MacKay Bayesian change-point.

items.design: Single full pipeline run with all three components active (alpha=0.9, SD-007 reafference enabled, SD-003 attribution pipeline). PASS: event_selectivity_margin > 0.05 (SD-008), world_forward_r2 > 0.8 (SD-007), attribution_gap > 0.02 (SD-003). This is an engineering integration check, not a new scientific claim.
items.notes: EXQ-023 originally attempted this combined test but failed because SD-007 input was wrong (z_self instead of z_world_raw_prev). All three components are now independently validated. Lower priority than ARC-007, ARC-029, MECH-104 pending experiments.
items.objective: V3 core stack integration test: verify SD-008 + SD-007 + SD-003 compose correctly in a single pipeline run now that all three are validated individually. Test that alpha_world=0.9 + reafference correction (z_world_raw_prev input) + counterfactual attribution pipeline together produce expected event selectivity, harm calibration, and attribution accuracy.

Test that cyclically-forced uncommitted (default/reflective) windows show higher z_world variance and E3 uncertainty than committed inter-sweep intervals, using a breath-oscillator approach that directly instantiates MECH-108 in current substrate.
items.design: Add BreathOscillator to MultiRateClock: every breath_period steps (e.g., 50 steps = ~0.2 Hz), briefly raise commit_threshold by sweep_amplitude for sweep_duration steps, creating a periodic uncommitted window within a single trained agent. Conditions: (A) oscillator active -- trained agent with periodic sweep forcing, (B) oscillator inactive -- same trained agent, no sweep (control). Within condition A, label steps as sweep-phase (uncommitted) vs inter-sweep (committed). PASS: sweep-phase z_world_var > inter-sweep z_world_var * 1.1 (C1); sweep-phase e3_running_var > inter-sweep * 1.05 (C2); >=20 sweep-phase steps observed (C3); E3 not collapsed outside sweeps: harm_pred_std > 0.01 (C4); no fatal errors (C5). Parameters: breath_period=50, sweep_amplitude=0.1, sweep_duration=20.
items.why_now: EXQ-065 design-inconclusive (always committed). Fresh-agent two-condition was the prior fix proposal. Cyclical oscillator is superior: (1) tests dynamics within single trained agent, not static comparison; (2) directly approximates MECH-108 respiratory sweep clock; (3) period/amplitude are falsifiable parameters linking experiment to biology. LIT-0094 will determine physiologically-grounded period/amplitude. Substrate: BreathOscillator extends existing MultiRateClock (heartbeat/clock.py).
items.notes: BreathOscillator implementation: add sweep_active flag to MultiRateClock; when True for sweep_duration steps, agent reads elevated commit_threshold (base + sweep_amplitude). Clock.py already has beta-modulated E3 rate (MECH-093) -- same pattern. If LIT-0094 returns clear empirical frequency data, update breath_period to match. Future substrate: wanting/liking Go/NoGo signals and full MECH-108 respiratory rhythm should replace oscillator injection naturally.
items.lit_grounding: LIT-0094: breath_period=50 confirmed (Zelano 0.16-0.33 Hz -> 30-60 steps). sweep_duration revised 5->20 steps (inhalation phase ~2 sec at 10 Hz simulation). Phase direction: inspiration lowers decision boundary (Braendholt 2025) -- BreathOscillator lowers commit_threshold by sweep_amplitude during sweep window. Dual mechanism confirmed (threshold + valence bias independent); valence channel deferred.

items.objective: Test that during oscillator-forced uncommitted windows, E3 shows elevated preparedness (differential gate elevation) under threat vs neutral conditions -- the core MECH-026 ready vigilance prediction, measured within breath-sweep phases.
items.design: Same BreathOscillator as EXP-0089 (breath_period=50, sweep_amplitude=0.1, sweep_duration=20). Two-environment crossed: threat env (harm sources present, dist 3-5 cells) vs neutral env (no harm sources). Within each env, label steps as sweep-phase (oscillator-forced uncommitted) vs inter-sweep (committed). PASS: readiness_gap (mean_beta_sweep_threat > mean_beta_sweep_neutral) > 0.005 (C1); threat does not force premature exit of sweep-phase: commit_rate_threat_sweep < 0.95 (C2); >=20 sweep steps in threat env (C3); harm_pred_std > 0.01 (C4); no fatal errors (C5). Parameters match EXP-0089 for cross-experiment comparability.
items.why_now: EXQ-067 design-inconclusive (uncommitted_threat_steps=0). Cyclical oscillator creates the uncommitted windows needed to test MECH-026. Shared oscillator infrastructure with EXP-0089 means implementation is a single substrate addition. The oscillator exposes MECH-026 within realistic within-agent dynamics rather than requiring state-matched fresh agents.
items.notes: Readiness gap is measured exclusively within sweep-phase steps to avoid conflation with committed-mode threat response (which is a different phenomenon). The claim is about vigilance as an uncommitted-but-primed state -- not about commitment rate under threat. If sweep-phase threat shows elevated beta AND prevents premature re-commitment (C2), that is MECH-026 confirmed. Future: full MECH-108 respiratory rhythm replaces oscillator; MECH-026 then measurable in natural breath-phase uncommitted windows.
items.lit_grounding: LIT-0094: breath_period=50 confirmed (Zelano 0.16-0.33 Hz -> 30-60 steps). sweep_duration revised 5->20 steps (inhalation phase ~2 sec at 10 Hz simulation). Phase direction: inspiration lowers decision boundary (Braendholt 2025) -- BreathOscillator lowers commit_threshold by sweep_amplitude during sweep window. Dual mechanism confirmed (threshold + valence bias independent); valence channel deferred.

items.objective: Test that after each oscillator-forced sweep release, commitment re-establishes more slowly in high-hazard vs low-hazard environment -- the MECH-047 hysteresis prediction, measured as within-oscillation re-commitment latency across environments.
items.design: Same BreathOscillator (breath_period=50, sweep_amplitude=0.1, sweep_duration=20). Two-environment: high-hazard (drift=0.4, interval=3 -- volatile) vs low-hazard (drift=0.0). Both agents trained to convergence with oscillator active. Measure: commitment_reestablishment_steps = mean steps from sweep-phase end (oscillator off) until running_variance returns below commit_threshold. PASS: high_hazard_reestablishment_steps > low_hazard_reestablishment_steps + epsilon (C1, hysteresis); oscillator produces measurable uncommitted windows in both envs: n_sweep_steps >= 20 (C2); high_hazard_commit_rate > low_hazard_commit_rate (C3, hazard level affects commitment baseline); no fatal errors (C4). Epsilon = 2 steps (conservative hysteresis threshold).
items.why_now: EXQ-068 design-inconclusive (always committed, no mode transitions). Cyclical oscillator creates the mode transitions needed to measure re-commitment latency each cycle. The hysteresis signal is the difference in recovery time across environments per cycle -- a cleaner test than Phase A/B checkpoint because it repeats within a single episode and provides many measurements rather than one.
items.notes: Hysteresis prediction: in high-hazard env, the agent has established stronger commitment priors, so re-commitment after sweep should be faster (stronger pull back to committed). OR: high-hazard makes re-commitment slower because each release reveals more threat signal. The direction is architecturally ambiguous -- LIT-0094 may clarify. Register as bidirectional (PASS on any significant difference). Future substrate: MECH-108 natural breath sweeps + valence signals should modulate the re-commitment rate endogenously, removing need for oscillator injection.
items.lit_grounding: LIT-0094: breath_period=50 confirmed (Zelano 0.16-0.33 Hz -> 30-60 steps). sweep_duration revised 5->20 steps (inhalation phase ~2 sec at 10 Hz simulation). Phase direction: inspiration lowers decision boundary (Braendholt 2025) -- BreathOscillator lowers commit_threshold by sweep_amplitude during sweep window. Dual mechanism confirmed (threshold + valence bias independent); valence channel deferred.

items.objective: Identify empirically-grounded parameter values for BreathOscillator (breath_period, sweep_amplitude, sweep_duration) and determine what biological signals cyclically modulate neural precision/commitment thresholds at ~0.2 Hz and slower frequencies.
key_questions: Does respiratory rhythm (exhalation phase specifically) cyclically modulate hippocampal, PFC, or BG commitment signals? If so, what is the phase relationship and amplitude?
What other slow oscillators cyclically modulate neural gain/precision at <1 Hz (infraslow, ultradian, circadian sub-bands)?
Is there direct evidence for exhalation-linked prefrontal or BG state-switching (not just oscillatory coupling, but actual commitment/exploration mode transitions)?
Does voluntary breath control (sighing, breath-holding) demonstrably modulate planning or decision commitment in behavioural paradigms?
What neuromodulators (NE, ACh, DA, serotonin) oscillate at respiratory or slower timescales and are linked to precision/gain modulation?
target_literature: Zelano et al. (2016) Nasal respiration entrains human limbic oscillations and modulates cognitive function. Nature Neuroscience.
Karalis & Bhatt (2020) Breathing coordinates cortico-hippocampal dynamics in mice during offline states. Nature Neuroscience.
Helfrich et al. (2018) Neural signatures of sleep-dependent memory consolidation in humans: a high-density sleep EEG and fMRI study.
Tort et al. (2018) Parallel detection of theta and respiration-coupled oscillations throughout the mouse brain. Scientific Reports.
Vlemincx et al. (2010) Sigh rate and respiratory variability during mental load and sustained attention. Acta Psychologica.
Yackle et al. (2017) Breathing control center neurons that promote arousal in mice. Science. (Pre-Botzinger --> LC link)
Palva et al. (2005) Infraslow EEG fluctuations as a surrogate activity for BOLD fluctuations.
Aston-Jones & Cohen (2005) An integrative theory of locus coeruleus-norepinephrine function. Annual Review of Neuroscience.
Lottem et al. (2018) Activation of serotonin neurons promotes active persistence in a probabilistic foraging task. Nature Communications.
Dayan & Yu (2006) Phasic norepinephrine: a neural interrupt signal for unexpected events.

mapping_to_claims.MECH-108: Empirical ~0.2 Hz respiratory coupling data constrains breath_period parameter.
mapping_to_claims.MECH-107: Exhalation-phase specificity -- does sweep occur on exhale or inhale?
mapping_to_claims.MECH-109: Voluntary modulation evidence -- behavioural studies of breath control and decision commitment.
mapping_to_claims.MECH-026: Ready vigilance -- does threat modulate respiratory rate or exhalation timing?
mapping_to_claims.MECH-029: Default mode -- is DMN activity phase-locked to respiratory cycle?
mapping_to_claims.MECH-047: Hysteresis -- does commitment persist across breath cycles differentially under high vs low stakes?

items.notes: This pull directly constrains EXP-0089/090/091 (BreathOscillator parameter values). If respiratory coupling literature provides clear phase/amplitude data, update breath_period and sweep_amplitude to physiologically-grounded values before running experiments. Also check for infraslow (<0.1 Hz) oscillators that might provide slower cycling -- these could be relevant for longer-timescale commitment hysteresis (MECH-047). Particular interest: any evidence that sigh rate or breath-pause frequency predicts behavioural plan-abandonment or decision revision events.
